agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Smart Route Optimization automates the assignment and dynamic recalculation of delivery routes for drivers in a logistics/courier platform. It spans multiple bounded contexts: route planning, fleet management, delivery lifecycle, customer communication, and operations configuration. The feature aims to reduce manual dispatching, improve delivery density, and keep customers informed in real-time.",
    "bounded_context": "This feature crosses at least four bounded contexts: (1) Route Planning — owns route calculation, recalculation triggers, and stop sequencing; (2) Fleet Management — owns driver profiles, shift lifecycle, vehicle classification, and zone assignments; (3) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle (pending, in-transit, failed, completed) and zone membership; (4) Customer Notification — owns notification triggers and delivery-window communication. Operations Configuration is a supporting context that provides zone-level parameter overrides.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Route", "definition": "An ordered sequence of delivery stops assigned to a driver for a single shift, produced by the optimization algorithm." },
      { "term": "Zone", "definition": "A geographic partition of the service area. Drivers are assigned to zones; deliveries belong to zones. A zone is the unit of operational configuration." },
      { "term": "Shift", "definition": "A bounded working period for a driver, beginning when the driver clocks in and ending when they clock out. A route is scoped to exactly one shift." },
      { "term": "Pending Delivery", "definition": "A delivery that has been created and is awaiting assignment to a driver's route. Only pending deliveries in the driver's zone are candidates for route assignment." },
      { "term": "Vehicle Class", "definition": "A classification of vehicle capability (e.g., bicycle, motorcycle, van, truck/freight). Each driver is licensed for one or more vehicle classes; each delivery requires a minimum vehicle class." },
      { "term": "Route Recalculation", "definition": "The act of re-running the optimization algorithm on a driver's current route, triggered by specific domain events. Produces a new stop sequence that replaces the previous one." },
      { "term": "Next-3-Stops Window", "definition": "A rolling notification boundary: when a delivery is among the next three stops on the active route, the customer is notified that delivery is imminent." },
      { "term": "Stop Time Estimate", "definition": "The expected duration a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes system-wide but is overridable per zone by the operations team." },
      { "term": "Manual Deviation", "definition": "When a driver's actual behavior diverges from the prescribed route sequence — e.g., skipping a stop, visiting stops out of order, or traveling off the planned path." },
      { "term": "Failed Delivery", "definition": "A delivery attempt that did not result in successful handoff to the recipient. Triggers route recalculation to remove or reschedule the stop." },
      { "term": "Licensed Vehicle Class", "definition": "The set of vehicle classes a driver is legally and operationally certified to operate. This is a property of the driver, not the vehicle currently in use." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "When a driver starts their shift, the system must automatically calculate and assign an optimized route from all pending deliveries in the driver's zone.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an operational automation policy. A dispatcher could manually assign routes in a degraded mode. The business chose automation but it is not a data-integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver starting a shift with N pending deliveries in their zone, an optimized route containing all eligible deliveries is assigned within the shift-start workflow, before the driver receives their first stop."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "A driver must NEVER be assigned a delivery that requires a vehicle class exceeding their licensed vehicle class(es).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a legal and safety constraint. A motorcycle courier physically cannot carry freight, and operating outside one's license is a regulatory violation. If this rule is violated, the system state is corrupt — it has created an undeliverable and potentially illegal assignment.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery on every active route, delivery.requiredVehicleClass ∈ driver.licensedVehicleClasses. No assignment pathway (auto or manual) may violate this."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Route recalculation must be triggered when a new delivery is added to the driver's zone while they have an active route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an optimization policy. Running on a stale route is suboptimal but does not corrupt state. The business could change the trigger frequency or batch recalculations.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a new pending delivery is created in zone Z, all drivers with active routes in zone Z have their routes flagged for recalculation and recalculated before the driver reaches their next stop (or within a bounded time window)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Route recalculation must be triggered when a delivery on the active route is marked as failed.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Policy decision to keep routes optimal. A stale route containing a failed stop is inefficient but not a state corruption.",
      "testable_assertion": "When delivery D on driver's active route transitions to 'failed', the route is recalculated with D removed from the remaining stops."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Route recalculation must be triggered when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Optimization policy. The definition of 'deviation' itself is configurable (threshold, detection method).",
      "testable_assertion": "When the system detects a driver deviation event (skip, reorder, or GPS off-path beyond threshold), a route recalculation is initiated using the driver's current position and remaining undelivered stops."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Customers must be notified when their delivery enters the next-3-stops window on the driver's active route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Notification policy. The window size (3) is a business preference, not a physical or legal constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a delivery transitions from position > 3 to position ≤ 3 on the active route (or starts at position ≤ 3), a notification is sent to the associated customer exactly once per route-window entry."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The default estimated stop time is 4 minutes per delivery, but the operations team can override this value per zone.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Operational parameter. The spec explicitly states it is overridable — this is a soft default, not a constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Route time calculations use the zone-specific stop time override if one exists; otherwise they fall back to 4 minutes. The override is scoped per zone and applied to all routes within that zone."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "A driver's route may only contain deliveries within their assigned zone.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Zone boundaries define operational, insurance, and regulatory scope. Assigning out-of-zone deliveries breaks the zone partitioning invariant and can cause coverage gaps, SLA violations, and insurance issues. If a delivery appears on a route outside its zone, the assignment is corrupt.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery on a driver's active route: delivery.zone == driver.assignedZone."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver starts their shift and there are 12 pending deliveries in their assigned zone",
      "when": "The shift-start event is processed",
      "then": "An optimized route containing all 12 eligible deliveries (filtered by vehicle class compatibility) is assigned to the driver before they receive navigation instructions"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A driver with an active route of 8 remaining stops",
      "when": "A new pending delivery is created in the driver's zone and is compatible with their vehicle class",
      "then": "The route is recalculated to include the new delivery, and the driver receives an updated stop sequence"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A driver with an active route attempts delivery at stop 3 of 10",
      "when": "The delivery at stop 3 is marked as failed",
      "then": "The route is recalculated with the failed delivery removed, and the driver receives an updated sequence of 9 (or fewer) remaining stops"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver with an active route who skips stop 2 and proceeds to stop 4",
      "when": "The system detects the deviation",
      "then": "The route is recalculated from the driver's current position with all remaining undelivered stops, and the driver receives an updated sequence"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver's active route has deliveries at positions 1 through 8, and the customer for delivery at position 4 has not yet been notified",
      "when": "The driver completes delivery at position 1, causing position 4 to become position 3",
      "then": "The customer for the delivery now at position 3 receives an 'arriving soon' notification"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A motorcycle-licensed driver in zone A and a pending freight delivery in zone A",
      "when": "Route calculation runs for that driver",
      "then": "The freight delivery is excluded from the driver's route; it remains in the pending pool for a driver with a compatible vehicle class"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "Zone B has an operations override setting stop time to 6 minutes",
      "when": "A route is calculated for a driver in zone B with 10 stops",
      "then": "The route's total estimated time uses 6 minutes per stop (60 minutes of stop time), not the default 4 minutes"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A route recalculation is triggered and a customer was already notified they are in the next-3-stops window",
      "when": "The recalculated route moves that customer's delivery to position 5 (outside the window)",
      "then": "The customer receives an updated notification that their delivery window has changed, preserving trust and accuracy"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Driver starts shift with zero pending deliveries in their zone", "expected_behavior": "Driver receives an empty route and enters an idle/waiting state. When the first delivery arrives in their zone, a route is created and assigned automatically." },
    { "scenario": "All pending deliveries in a zone require a vehicle class the available driver is not licensed for", "expected_behavior": "Driver receives an empty route. Deliveries remain pending. The system should surface an alert to dispatch/operations that unassignable deliveries exist in the zone." },
    { "scenario": "Route recalculation is triggered while a previous recalculation is still in progress", "expected_behavior": "The system must serialize or debounce recalculations per driver to prevent race conditions. The most recent trigger should win — earlier in-flight calculations should be discarded." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple recalculation triggers fire simultaneously (new delivery added + failed delivery in same zone)", "expected_behavior": "A single recalculation incorporating both changes should be executed, not two sequential recalculations." },
    { "scenario": "Customer is notified at position 3, then route recalculation moves them back to position 5, then another recalculation moves them back to position 2", "expected_behavior": "Customer should not receive a duplicate 'arriving soon' notification. The system must track notification state per delivery per route lifecycle." },
    { "scenario": "Driver deviates, recalculation runs, driver deviates again from the new route immediately", "expected_behavior": "Second deviation triggers another recalculation. A rate-limiting mechanism should prevent infinite recalculation loops (e.g., cooldown period or max recalculations per time window)." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery is reassigned from one zone to another while a driver in the original zone has it on their active route", "expected_behavior": "The delivery must be removed from the original driver's route (triggering recalculation) and appear as pending in the new zone." },
    { "scenario": "Driver's shift ends while deliveries remain on route", "expected_behavior": "Undelivered stops revert to pending status and become eligible for reassignment. Customers in the next-3-stops window should receive an updated notification." },
    { "scenario": "Operations team changes the zone stop-time override while drivers have active routes in that zone", "expected_behavior": "Existing routes should use the updated stop time on the next recalculation. ETAs communicated to customers should eventually reflect the new estimate." },
    { "scenario": "Driver completes stops 1 and 2, is now on stop 3 — a recalculation fires and reorders remaining stops", "expected_behavior": "Completed stops must never reappear on the recalculated route. Only undelivered stops are eligible for resequencing." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Optimized routing directly reduces fuel costs, increases deliveries-per-shift (higher revenue density), and reduces overtime. Vehicle class enforcement prevents costly failed delivery attempts and potential regulatory fines. The 4-minute configurable stop time allows zone-level tuning that can improve ETA accuracy and reduce customer compensation claims.",
    "operational": "Eliminates manual dispatching for the happy path, freeing dispatchers to handle exceptions. Dynamic recalculation keeps routes efficient as conditions change. However, introduces operational complexity: the recalculation engine becomes a critical path dependency — if it fails, drivers have no route. Requires a fallback (manual dispatch or last-known-good route).",
    "user": "Drivers get clear, optimized instructions reducing cognitive load and navigation errors. Customers get proactive 'arriving soon' notifications improving satisfaction and reducing 'where is my package' support calls. Risk: frequent recalculations may frustrate drivers if the route changes constantly mid-shift.",
    "risk": "High dependency on route optimization engine availability. Vehicle class mismatch is a legal/safety risk if the invariant is not enforced at every assignment pathway (including manual overrides). Notification accuracy is a brand trust risk — notifying customers then pushing them back in the queue erodes confidence. Recalculation storms under high delivery volume could degrade system performance.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers", "impact": "Primary users. Gain automated routing but lose some autonomy. Frequent recalculations may cause frustration. Vehicle class enforcement protects them from impossible assignments." },
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Receive proactive delivery notifications. Risk of notification whiplash if recalculations frequently change their position. Improved ETA accuracy overall." },
      { "group": "Operations Team", "impact": "Gain zone-level configuration control. Reduced manual dispatching burden. Must monitor for edge cases (empty zones, unassignable deliveries). New operational runbook needed for optimization engine failures." },
      { "group": "Dispatchers", "impact": "Role shifts from manual route creation to exception handling. Need visibility into why the algorithm made specific decisions. May need override capability." },
      { "group": "Finance/Compliance", "impact": "Vehicle class enforcement is a compliance requirement. Routing efficiency directly impacts cost-per-delivery KPI." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 52,
    "reasoning": "The spec has several significant ambiguities that prevent high confidence: (1) 'zone' assignment mechanics are undefined — is a driver statically assigned or dynamically placed? (2) 'manual deviation' lacks a precise detection definition (GPS threshold? Skip detection? Both?). (3) The interaction between recalculation and already-sent notifications is unspecified. (4) No mention of route optimization algorithm constraints (shortest distance? fewest turns? time windows?). (5) No fallback behavior specified when the optimization engine is unavailable. These ambiguities affect core acceptance criteria.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Vehicle class enforcement is a hard invariant (legal/safety)",
      "Recalculation triggers are clearly enumerated (3 events)",
      "Stop time default and override mechanism is well-specified",
      "Next-3-stops notification trigger is clear in concept"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Definition and detection of 'manual deviation'",
      "Zone assignment mechanics (static vs dynamic, single vs multi-zone)",
      "Notification lifecycle when recalculation changes stop order",
      "Optimization algorithm objectives and constraints",
      "Concurrency behavior under rapid successive recalculation triggers",
      "Fallback behavior when optimization engine is unavailable"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift",
      "Zone membership of a delivery is determined at creation time and is stable",
      "Vehicle class compatibility is a strict subset check, not a ranking",
      "The 'next 3 stops' window is based on sequence position, not time or distance",
      "Route recalculation replaces the entire remaining sequence, not just inserts/removes one stop",
      "Completed deliveries are never re-added to a route during recalculation"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec does not define what constitutes a 'manual deviation.' This could mean: (A) GPS position diverges from planned path beyond a distance threshold, (B) driver marks a stop as skipped, (C) driver completes stops out of the prescribed sequence, or (D) all of the above. Each interpretation has different detection mechanisms, latency characteristics, and recalculation frequency implications.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team", "Engineering"],
      "options": ["A: GPS-only deviation with configurable distance threshold", "B: Explicit skip/reorder actions only", "C: Both GPS and explicit actions (recommended)", "D: Let operations configure which deviation types trigger recalculation per zone"],
      "recommendation": "Option C — detect both GPS deviation and explicit skip/reorder, as they represent fundamentally different driver behaviors that both invalidate the current route."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The notification lifecycle when recalculation changes stop order is unspecified. If a customer was notified they are in the 'next 3 stops' window, and recalculation pushes them out of that window, should they receive: (A) no update (stale but no noise), (B) a revised notification ('your delivery has been rescheduled'), or (C) only a new notification when they re-enter the window?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Product Team", "Customer Support"],
      "options": ["A: Silent — no correction notification", "B: Explicit correction notification", "C: Correction + re-notification on re-entry"],
      "recommendation": "Option B — customers who were told 'arriving soon' will be actively waiting. Silence erodes trust. A brief 'updated ETA' message manages expectations without notification spam."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "The spec does not define the optimization objective function. 'Optimized route' could mean: shortest total distance, shortest total time, earliest completion of high-priority deliveries, maximum deliveries per shift, or adherence to customer time windows. Without this, the algorithm cannot be validated as 'correct.'",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product Team", "Drivers", "Customers", "Engineering"],
      "options": ["Shortest distance", "Shortest time (accounting for traffic)", "Priority-weighted time minimization", "Configurable per zone by operations"],
      "recommendation": "Shortest time with priority weighting — pure distance optimization ignores traffic and delivery priority. Product team must define the objective explicitly."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No fallback behavior is specified for when the route optimization engine is unavailable or times out. This is a critical-path dependency: if it fails on shift start, the driver has no route. If it fails on recalculation, the driver has a stale route.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Fallback to manual dispatch", "Use last-known-good route on recalculation failure, FIFO ordering on shift-start failure", "Queue the driver in a waiting state until the engine recovers"],
      "recommendation": "Use last-known-good route for recalculation failures. For shift-start failures, fall back to naive geographic ordering (nearest-neighbor) so the driver can begin working while the system recovers."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
